reasoning_model_1: The model generates a distinct non-linguistic sigh sound at the very beginning (0:00-0:02), sounding natural and breathy. However, the subsequent spoken content ('The movie was so boring. I fell asleep halfway through it.') is completely different from the requested script ('Sigh, this proposal has been revised countless times...'). The model seemingly hallucinated a different sentence entirely or was generated based on a different prompt than the one provided in the evaluation instructions. According to the evaluation criteria, if the content is clearly inconsistent with the script, the score must be 0.
reasoning_model_2: The model accurately follows the text script provided in the prompt. However, regarding the 'Non-Linguistic Vocalizations' feature, the model fails to convert the text marker 'Sigh' into an actual non-linguistic sound. Instead, at 0:00-0:02, it linguistically reads the word 'Sigh' (pronounced /saɪ/) in a stretched, dramatic manner. While the prosody of the rest of the sentence (0:02-0:14) conveys the requested frustration and tiredness, the failure to produce the specific non-linguistic sound (breathing out) and instead verbalizing the stage direction constitutes a failure in the specific paralinguistic dimension requested.
model_comparison: Model 1 failed fundamentally on content accuracy. The audio content provided ('The movie was so boring...') has no relation to the requested text ('This proposal has been revised...'). Therefore, despite producing a good sigh sound, it is unusable and receives a 0. Model 2 followed the content accurately but failed to interpret the instruction 'with a sigh' and the text 'Sigh' as a command to produce a sound effect; instead, it read the word 'Sigh' verbatim. However, because Model 2 provided the correct message and Model 1 provided completely wrong content, Model 2 is the winner. Model 2's failure is a feature control issue (reading stage directions), whereas Model 1's failure is a critical hallucination.
